Snipara's tech stack (from documentation):

- Frontend: Next.js 14, Tailwind, DaisyUI, Tremor  
- Backend: Next.js API Routes, tRPC

Snipara’s core value proposition is Context Optimization: dramatically reducing documentation to highly relevant context so clients can run their own LLMs efficiently. Key points (from the docs):

- “90% context reduction — From 500K tokens to ~5K tokens of highly relevant content”  
- Clients “use their own LLM — No vendor lock‑in, no LLM costs for us”  
- “High margins — Pure compute, no LLM costs passed through”

(Also: the key insight: the real value is the optimized context, not the LLM response.)

Snipara’s core value proposition is Context Optimization as a Service: dramatically reduce context (≈90% reduction — from ~500K tokens to ~5K highly relevant tokens), let clients use their own LLM (no vendor lock‑in, no LLM costs passed through), and provide token budgeting/highly relevant context for client LLMs.

- 90% context reduction — from 500K tokens to ~5K tokens of highly relevant content  
- Client uses their own LLM — no vendor lock‑in, no LLM costs for Snipara  
- High margins — pure compute; no LLM costs passed through

- Token counting: sections are counted with tiktoken using the cl100k_base encoder (GPT-4/Claude compatible).  
- Selection algorithm: candidate sections are ranked by relevance and selected greedily (highest relevance first) until the token budget (max_tokens) would be exceeded. If a full section doesn't fit but space remains, a partial section is included using smart_truncate() to fit the remaining tokens.  
- Smart truncation: truncation cuts at a sentence boundary (. ! ?), appends "..." and marks the section as truncated in the metadata.  
- Budget allocation: the system supports category/project allocations (examples in docs: CATEGORY_BUDGETS like MANDATORY: 0.4, BEST_PRACTICES: 0.3, etc., and DEFAULT_ALLOCATION with project: 0.40 plus shared categories). Allocation routines allocate per-category or project budgets and fill them by relevance (allocateWithinBudget sorts by relevance and includes docs until the category budget is used).  
- Session & system context: session context can be included (implementation notes: session context inclusion up to 20% of budget) and system instructions can be auto-injected.  
- Response metadata: returned sections include file path, line ranges, relevance_score, token_count; overall response contains sections, total_tokens, truncated flag, session_context and suggestions.  
- Implementation notes / behaviors: counting uses encoder.encode(section.content) to compute token counts; greedy selection stops when budget is full; multi-query and merged/shared-context flows distribute budgets per-query or per-collection as shown in the docs.

- Token counting: Snipara uses tiktoken (cl100k_base encoder) to count tokens for documents/sections.

- Context selection: candidate sections are ranked by relevance and then greedily selected (highest relevance first) until the response token budget (max_tokens, default 4000) would be exceeded.

- Partial sections: if a full section would exceed the remaining budget, Snipara applies smart_truncate() to fit the remaining tokens, truncating at sentence boundaries (. ! ?) and appending "..." — the section metadata is marked truncated: true.

- Metadata and accounting: selected sections include file path, line ranges, relevance_score and token_count; the response returns sections, total_tokens, truncated flag, session_context (if any), and suggestions.

- Session context: previous/session context may be injected and is accounted for (implementation notes: up to 20% of the budget).

- Allocation strategies: there are default budget allocations and priorities (examples: CATEGORY_BUDGETS like MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10%; project-specific allocation prioritized). The system can allocate budget by project vs shared collections and by category, allocating project docs first and then shared categories using configured percentages.

- Hard limits & enforcement examples: limits such as MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION and per-collection/document truncation logic are enforced when needed.

- Outputs & metrics: tools return total token usage and token-efficiency metrics (e.g., compression_ratio, token_reduction_pct, cost_savings_pct) and include usage/search telemetry (search_time_ms, sections_scanned).

(Quoted/paraphrased from the provided documentation context.)

- Token counting: Snipara uses tiktoken (cl100k_base encoder) to count tokens for documents/sections precisely.

- Budget target: tools (e.g., rlm_context_query) accept a max_tokens budget (default 4000). Session context can be included and is limited (implementation notes: up to 20% of the budget).

- Selection algorithm:
  - Candidates are ranked by relevance.
  - Greedy selection: include highest-relevance sections first while summing token counts.
  - Stop when adding the next full section would exceed the budget.

- Partial sections / smart truncation:
  - If only part of a section fits, Snipara uses smart_truncate() to cut the content to the remaining tokens.
  - Truncation happens at sentence boundaries (. ! ?) and appends "..."; the section metadata gets truncated: true.

- Metadata and reporting: selected sections return file path, line ranges, relevance_score, token_count; tool responses include sections list, total_tokens, truncated flag, session_context, and suggestions.

- Budget allocation across categories/sources:
  - There are default allocation priorities/percentages (examples in docs):
    - CATEGORY_BUDGETS example: MANDATORY 0.4, BEST_PRACTICES 0.3, GUIDELINES 0.2, REFERENCE 0.1.
    - DEFAULT_ALLOCATION example (project + shared): project 0.40, MANDATORY 0.25, BEST_PRACTICES 0.20, GUIDELINES 0.10, REFERENCE 0.05.
  - Project docs are allocated first; shared docs are allocated by category. Within each bucket, docs are sorted by relevance and allocated until the bucket budget is exhausted.

- Hard limits / enforcement: code enforces collection and token limits (e.g., max tokens per collection), truncating or excluding docs to meet those limits.

- Metrics & tools: token-efficiency metrics (compression_ratio, token_reduction_pct, cost_savings_pct) and APIs/tools (rlm_context_query, allocateTokenBudget) expose token usage and allocation results.

(Quoted/paraphrased from the provided documentation.)

"Shared Context Collections allow teams to create reusable coding standards, best practices, and guidelines that are automatically available to all team projects."

How they work (concise):
- Creation: a Team Admin creates a Shared Context Collection and uploads documents (example API: rlm_upload_shared_document or POST /api/teams/[teamId]/context).
- Link to projects: collections are linked to projects in the dashboard; linked projects automatically inherit all documents in the collection.
- Retrieval: projects call rlm_shared_context to get the merged/shared documents (parameters include categories, max_tokens, etc.). rlm_list_templates and rlm_get_template are available for prompt templates stored in collections.
- Injection into queries: shared context is merged with project docs and is included automatically in rlm_context_query (shared context is queried first, then local project docs). Token budget is split between shared and project scope (examples show 40% shared / 60% project or a configurable shared_context_budget_percent such as 30%).
- Category priorities and budgets: documents are categorized and prioritized; categories (in priority order) are MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE with recommended token-budget allocations (40% / 30% / 20% / 10% respectively).
- Access: Team-wide shared collections require the Team plan (or Enterprise) — not available on Free/Pro plans.

- What they are: Shared Context Collections are team-owned collections of reusable rules, coding standards, best practices, guidelines and reference material that are made automatically available to all linked team projects. ("Shared Context Collections allow teams to create reusable coding standards, best practices, and guidelines that are automatically available to all team projects.")

- Purpose: enforce consistent, team-wide conventions (security, architecture, style, templates, etc.) so every developer/AI session sees the same standards.

- Creation & publishing:
  1. Team admin creates a Shared Context Collection (dashboard).
  2. Upload documents (e.g., via rlm_upload_shared_document or POST /api/teams/[teamId]/context).
  3. Link the collection to projects (Project > Shared Context → Link Collection). Projects then automatically inherit the collection.

- How they’re used at query time:
  - rlm_shared_context merges all linked collections and returns categorized context (documents grouped by MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE).
  - When running rlm_context_query, shared context is loaded first (shared best practices are queried FIRST) and then local project docs are queried with the remaining token budget. You can control inclusion and budget (e.g., include_shared_context, shared_context_budget_percent).
  - Example tools: rlm_shared_context, rlm_list_templates, rlm_get_template; you can also inject shared context into a session with rlm_inject so subsequent queries automatically follow team standards.

- Categories & token budget: Documents are categorized and prioritized. The documented default allocation is MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10% (and documents have a 0–100 priority within category).

- Access / plan: Team-wide shared collections require the TEAM plan (or ENTERPRISE).

Shared Context Collections are team-managed collections of reusable rules, standards, templates and docs (coding standards, security rules, architecture patterns, etc.) that are linked to team projects and automatically included in queries so every developer and project uses the same conventions.

How they work (concise):

- Purpose: store team coding standards, best practices, guidelines and reference material for automatic inclusion in project queries.
- Create & populate:
  - Team admin creates a Shared Context Collection (dashboard).
  - Upload documents (e.g., rlm_upload_shared_document) into the collection with a category (MANDATORY | BEST_PRACTICES | GUIDELINES | REFERENCE) and priority.
- Link to projects:
  - Link the collection to projects via Project > Shared Context in the dashboard. Projects automatically inherit all documents from linked collections.
- Retrieval & injection:
  - Use rlm_shared_context to get merged team context from linked collections; the returned context is categorized (MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE).
  - You can inject that context into a session (rlm_inject) so it applies to subsequent queries.
  - rlm_context_query merges shared context with project docs automatically (shared context is queried first).
- Category priorities and token budgets:
  - Documents are prioritized by category and priority. Typical token-budget distribution shown in the docs: MANDATORY ~40%, BEST_PRACTICES ~30%, GUIDELINES ~20%, REFERENCE ~10% (examples in the docs show this allocation).
- Tools & templates:
  - Related tools: rlm_shared_context, rlm_list_templates, rlm_get_template (for prompt templates stored in collections).
- Access:
  - Team/Enterprise plans required (not available on Free/Pro).

- Overview: Snipara implements the OAuth Device Flow (RFC 8628). A CLI starts the flow; the user authenticates in a browser and the CLI polls the server for the token.

- Step 1 — start device auth: the client POSTs to /api/oauth/device/code with {"client_id":"snipara_cli"}. Example response includes:
  - device_code (opaque code the client uses to poll)
  - user_code (human-entered code, e.g. "ABCD-1234")
  - verification_uri (URL the user visits)
  - verification_uri_complete (URL prefilled with the code)
  - expires_in (e.g. 900)
  - interval (e.g. 5)

- Step 2 — user completes auth: the user visits verification_uri (or verification_uri_complete) in a browser and enters/approves the user_code.

- Step 3 — client polls for token: the client repeatedly POSTs to /api/oauth/device/token with:
  {
    "grant_type":"urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code",
    "device_code":"<device_code>",
    "client_id":"snipara_cli"
  }
  Responses: either {"error":"authorization_pending"} while waiting, or on success an access token payload:
  {
    "access_token":"snipara_at_...",
    "token_type":"Bearer",
    "expires_in":2592000,
    "refresh_token":"snipara_rt_..."
  }

- How tokens are used/stored:
  - Successful OAuth tokens are used as: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
  - The client checks for a stored OAuth token at ~/.snipara/tokens.json first; if none or not used, it falls back to API key auth (X-API-Key: rlm_...).

- Notes / troubleshooting:
  - Example device-code expiry and poll interval shown (expires_in: 900, interval: 5).
  - To bypass OAuth and force API-key usage, set SNIPARA_IGNORE_OAUTH=true (or remove ~/.snipara/tokens.json).

- Overview: Snipara uses the OAuth Device Flow (RFC 8628) so users can authenticate without copying API keys — "run a CLI command, visit a URL, enter a code, and authenticate via browser."

- Start flow (server endpoint): POST /api/oauth/device/code with body {"client_id":"snipara_cli"}. The response contains:
  - device_code (for the client to poll)
  - user_code (for the user to enter)
  - verification_uri (URL the user visits)
  - verification_uri_complete (URL pre-filled with the code)
  - expires_in (seconds until the device/user codes expire)
  - interval (recommended polling interval)

- User step: User visits verification_uri (or verification_uri_complete) in a browser and enters the user_code to approve access.

- Poll for token (server endpoint): Client polls POST /api/oauth/device/token with:
  - grant_type: "urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code"
  - device_code
  - client_id: "snipara_cli"
  - Pending response: {"error":"authorization_pending"}
  - Success response: includes access_token ("snipara_at_..."), token_type ("Bearer"), expires_in, and refresh_token ("snipara_rt_...").

- Usage & storage:
  - OAuth tokens are used in requests as: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
  - The MCP server checks for a stored OAuth token in ~/.snipara/tokens.json first, then falls back to an API key (X-API-Key: rlm_...) from environment variables.

- Snipara uses the OAuth Device Flow (RFC 8628) so users can authenticate without copying API keys: run a CLI command, visit a verification URL in a browser, and enter a user code.

- Step 1 — obtain device code:
  - POST /api/oauth/device/code with { "client_id": "snipara_cli" }  
  - Response includes device_code, user_code, verification_uri, verification_uri_complete, expires_in, interval.

- Step 2 — user authorizes in browser:
  - User visits verification_uri (or uses verification_uri_complete) and enters the user_code to authenticate.

- Step 3 — client polls for token:
  - POST /api/oauth/device/token with
    { "grant_type": "urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code", "device_code": "...", "client_id": "snipara_cli" }  
  - Polling returns "authorization_pending" until the user authorizes; on success returns access_token (snipara_at_...), token_type, expires_in, and refresh_token (snipara_rt_...).

- Usage and storage:
  - OAuth access tokens are sent as Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
  - The client checks ~/.snipara/tokens.json first for stored OAuth tokens, falling back to API key if none.

- Notes / alternatives:
  - You can force API-key authentication by setting SNIPARA_IGNORE_OAUTH=true (client skips ~/.snipara/tokens.json) or by removing ~/.snipara/tokens.json.

The main Prisma models are: User, Project, and TeamMember.

The project uses a strict layered architecture with one‑way imports (lower layers cannot import higher). As documented:

Import hierarchy:
```
Routes (app/api/)
  ↓ imports
Services (lib/services/) — External API adapters
  ↓ imports
Repositories (lib/db/queries/) — Centralized CRUD only
  ↓ imports
Utils & Types — No dependencies
```

Rules: route handlers are thin (auth → Zod validation → delegate to service/query → return response), all DB access must go through lib/db/queries (repository pattern), and types/utils must have no dependencies on other layers.

Snipara uses a strict layered architecture where lower layers cannot import from higher. The layers (top → bottom) are:

- Routes (app/api/) — thin HTTP handlers  
- Services (lib/services/) — external API adapters / business logic  
- Repositories (lib/db/queries/) — centralized CRUD only  
- Utils & Types — shared, no dependencies

Route handlers must be thin (authenticate, validate with Zod, delegate to service/query, return response).

Snipara uses a strict layered architecture:

- Import hierarchy: Routes (app/api/) → Services (lib/services/) → Repositories (lib/db/queries/) → Utils & Types (no dependencies).  
- Lower layers cannot import from higher layers.  
- Routes are thin handlers (authenticate, validate with Zod, delegate to service/query, return response).  
- All DB access goes through the repository layer (`lib/db/queries/`); routes must not call Prisma directly.

- How Snipara helps (from the docs):
  - "90% Context Reduction: From 500K tokens to ~5K relevant tokens" — RLM/Snipara finds and returns only the most relevant sections (token-budgeted) instead of the whole corpus.
  - It implements token budgeting (client specifies max_tokens), accurate token counting with tiktoken, greedy selection by relevance, and smart_truncate to fit remaining tokens.
  - Shared team context is injected first (with configurable budget percent) and session context can persist (up to budgeted allowance).
  - For queries that truly exceed a single window, recursive tools (rlm_decompose, rlm_multi_query) decompose the problem into sub-queries so the LLM can handle 500K+ docs via multiple targeted calls.

- Practical effect for a 500K-token codebase + 128K-window LLM:
  - Snipara reduces the context you actually send to the LLM to the optimized token set (the doc example: ~5K tokens), which easily fits inside a 128K window.
  - If you need coverage beyond one window, the recursive tools orchestrate multiple smaller queries rather than sending the whole 500K at once.

- Cost savings (from the docs):
  - Snipara itself "doesn't run inference, client uses their own LLM" — so you avoid additional provider inference costs from the service.
  - By drastically reducing the tokens you send to your LLM (doc example: from 500K → ~5K), you materially reduce the number of LLM tokens billed for each query, resulting in much lower LLM inference costs.

- How Snipara helps:
  - "90% Context Reduction: From 500K tokens to ~5K relevant tokens" — Snipara compacts a huge codebase into a small, relevant set of sections via its context-query tooling and token-budgeting (client specifies `max_tokens`).  
  - Token-budgeting + smart truncation: the engine counts tokens (tiktoken/cl100k_base), greedily selects highest-relevance sections up to your `max_tokens`, and uses smart_truncate() at sentence boundaries if needed.  
  - Shared/ session context and recursive tools: shared context is injected first and session context can persist; for extremely large/complex queries it provides rlm_decompose/rlm_multi_query to break work into sub-queries so you can handle 500K+ docs with limited LLM windows.
  - LLM-agnostic & fits your window: you control `max_tokens` so the returned context will fit inside your LLM’s context window (e.g., 128K).

- What this means for costs (per the docs):
  - You send far fewer tokens to your LLM — from ~500K down to ~5K (90% reduction) — so your billable LLM tokens are greatly reduced.  
  - Snipara “doesn't run inference, client uses their own LLM” — there are no extra inference costs from Snipara itself.  
  - Snipara pricing (context plans) is separate and small compared to ongoing LLM token costs: FREE $0 (100 queries/mo), PRO $19/mo (5,000 queries/mo), TEAM $49/mo (20,000 queries/mo), ENTERPRISE $499/mo (Unlimited).

Summary: Snipara compacts a 500K-token codebase into ~5K relevant tokens (or otherwise into whatever `max_tokens` you set), ensuring the context fits your 128K LLM window and dramatically cutting the number of LLM billable tokens; Snipara itself does not run inference and is priced via the listed context plans.

How Snipara helps (from the docs)
- “90% Context Reduction: From 500K tokens to ~5K relevant tokens.” Snipara’s context optimization returns a small set of highly relevant sections instead of the whole codebase.
- Token budgeting: clients set max_tokens and the engine greedily selects/ranks sections, uses tiktoken for accurate counts, and smart_truncate to fit the remaining budget.
- Recursive context tools (rlm_decompose / rlm_multi_query) let you decompose large queries and orchestrate multiple sub-queries so you can handle 500K+ docs even with limited LLM windows.
- Shared-context-first budgeting: shared team rules are loaded first (configurable percent), then project docs use the remaining budget.
- LLM-agnostic / no inference costs: “We don't run inference, client uses their own LLM,” so Snipara only prepares optimized context (you pay LLM inference for far fewer tokens).

What that means for a 500K → 128K scenario
- Instead of trying to feed hundreds of thousands of tokens, Snipara reduces the context sent to your LLM to the relevant ~5K-token payload (or whatever max_tokens you set), which fits easily inside a 128K window and avoids sending the full 500K.
- This reduces the number of tokens your LLM must process per request (and therefore reduces your LLM inference token usage).

Concrete dollar savings
- The documentation states the token reduction and that Snipara does not run inference, but it does not provide concrete monetary cost figures or exact cost-savings calculations. Not found in documentation.

- Use rlm_context_query when you want a full-featured documentation query / semantic search (the primary tool). The docs say: "rlm_context_query — Full-featured documentation query" and the workflow example says to call rlm_context_query first for questions like "How does authentication work?"

- Use rlm_decompose for complex, large, or multi-part questions that need to be broken into sub-queries and an execution plan. The docs describe rlm_decompose as: "Analyze query and return sub-queries with execution plan" and show it in the recommended flow for complex questions: "Complex question → rlm_decompose → rlm_multi_query". The POWER USER guidance also says to use rlm_decompose when you "want to inspect/modify sub-queries before running."

- Additional note (from docs): rlm_decompose produces sub-queries and an execution plan (it "doesn't return content"), which you then run (e.g., with rlm_multi_query) and synthesize.

Use rlm_context_query for normal documentation queries; use rlm_decompose when the question is complex and you need it split into sub-queries and an execution plan.

- rlm_context_query: "Full-featured documentation query (semantic, budgeted)" — the primary tool to ask "How does X work?" and for regular semantic searches and ranked results.  
- rlm_decompose: "Analyze query and return sub-queries with execution plan" — a Power/User/Pro+ tool to break complex questions into sub-queries, show dependencies and suggested sequence, and let you inspect/modify sub-queries before running (used as part of the recursive/near-infinite context workflow with rlm_multi_query).

- Use rlm_context_query when you want a full‑featured documentation query / semantic search — the default first choice for questions like "How does X work?" (docs: "Full-featured documentation query", "Use rlm_context_query first").

- Use rlm_decompose when the question is complex and should be split into sub‑queries and an execution plan — e.g., large, multi‑part research or "Explain the complete authentication system". rlm_decompose "decompose[s] a complex query into sub-queries" and is appropriate when you "want to inspect/modify sub-queries before running" or to enable recursive/near‑infinite context workflows (combine with rlm_multi_query to execute the sub-queries). Note: rlm_decompose returns a plan/sub-queries (it does not return document content itself).

- Looking for a specific function → keyword  
- "How does X work?" questions → semantic  
- General documentation search → hybrid  
- Error code lookup → keyword  
- Architecture understanding → semantic  
- API endpoint discovery → keyword  
- Best practices search → hybrid

Search mode brief descriptions (from docs):  
- keyword: basic term matching (Free)  
- semantic: embedding similarity (Pro+)  
- hybrid: combined (best results) (Pro+)

Use the table in "When to Use Each Mode":

- Looking for specific function → keyword  
- "How does X work?" questions → semantic  
- General documentation search → hybrid

What is documented (pieces you can assemble):

- Claude Desktop starts an MCP client by running the configured command. Example config:
  - command: "uvx", args: ["snipara-mcp"]
  - env: SNIPARA_API_KEY, SNIPARA_PROJECT_ID

- The MCP server code lives in apps/mcp-server/src/ and changes must be dual-pushed (RLMSaas repo → Vercel+PyPI, and snipara-fastapi repo → Railway). Files listed as requiring sync include server.py, db.py, auth.py, rlm_engine.py, mcp_transport.py, usage.py, config.py, models.py, services/*.py — indicating these files participate in request handling, DB access, and auth.

- Claude Code/Claude Desktop integration uses hooks around tool use:
  - PreToolUse/PostToolUse/PreCompact/SessionStart hooks call rlm-hook commands (e.g., "rlm-hook pre-tool") to prepare and record tool interactions.

- Snipara exposes RLM-compatible tools (via snipara-mcp / rlm_tools.py):
  - Examples: context_query (primary), sections, search, read, shared_context, etc. A client/request can call these tools to retrieve optimized context or read data.

- RLM orchestration pattern (example): the model calls Snipara context tools to locate relevant data, then executes a REPL (sandboxed code) to parse/transform raw data and returns a compact JSON result to the model. This shows a tool→REPL→result pattern rather than streaming raw data.

What is missing (not documented)
- A single, explicit end-to-end sequence that ties Claude Desktop → MCP client process → network protocol details → specific MCP server endpoints → authentication handshake → exact database queries (SQL/ORM), DB connection flow, and response formatting is not provided. The precise request/response messages, routing inside server.py/db.py, and how results are returned to Claude Desktop are not described.

If you want a full end-to-end trace, the documentation does not provide it — you would need the server.py/db.py/auth.py implementations or an architecture diagram (not included here).

What is documented (relevant pieces you can chain together):

- Claude Desktop starts the MCP client:
  - Add a mcpServers entry that runs the local command "uvx" with args ["snipara-mcp"] and provides SNIPARA_API_KEY and SNIPARA_PROJECT_ID in env (example file path and JSON shown).

- Deployment / server endpoints:
  - The MCP server code lives in apps/mcp-server/src and must be pushed to two repos:
    1) RLMSaas repo → deploys to Vercel + PyPI
    2) snipara-fastapi repo → deploys to api.snipara.com (Railway)
  - Files implementing server, DB, and auth include server.py, db.py, auth.py, rlm_engine.py, etc. (listed as "Files requiring dual-repo sync").

- Hooks / automation (Claude Code full automation):
  - PreToolUse hook runs an rlm-hook pre-tool command; PostToolUse runs rlm-hook post-tool; PreCompact and SessionStart hooks call rlm-hook commands (sample .claude/settings.json provided).

- RLM Runtime / Snipara integration:
  - rlm-runtime includes a Snipara integration: snipara-mcp exposes tools and includes a SniparaClient for HTTP API calls.
  - snipara-mcp v1.4.0 exports tools such as context_query, sections, search, read, etc., which are the programmatic interfaces an orchestrator or client would call.

- First-query behavior:
  - The first rlm_context_query in a session includes tool tips and session context; subsequent queries do not. Response field first_query_tips_included is true only on first query.

Putting those pieces together (documented facts only):
- Claude Desktop is configured to run the local "snipara-mcp" command with API key/project env — that launches the MCP client on the user machine.
- That client can use hooks (PreToolUse/PostToolUse) to call rlm-hook commands as it runs.
- The MCP server implementation (server.py, db.py, auth.py, etc.) is deployed to Vercel and to api.snipara.com; the code handling incoming API calls and database interactions resides in those files (names given), and must be synchronized between repos.
- Programmatic access to Snipara (used by runtimes/tools) is via SniparaClient (HTTP API) and the exported tools (context_query, read, search, etc.).

No explicit end-to-end sequence showing the exact request/HTTP endpoints, authentication handshake, database query statements, or the response mapping from DB back to Claude Desktop is provided in the documentation excerpt.

Options (from the docs)
- Project API Key (header: X-API-Key: rlm_...)
- Team API Key (header: X-API-Key: rlm_...)
- OAuth access token (header: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...) — obtained via OAuth Device Flow (RFC 8628)

How they differ in security (paraphrased / quoted from docs)
- API keys
  - Format and storage: keys use the rlm_ prefix and are SHA-256 hashed in the database ("Key format | `rlm_` prefix, SHA-256 hashed in DB").
  - Logging/UI: only prefix-only in logs and only the first 8 chars are shown ("Only first 8 chars shown in UI/logs"; "prefix-only in logs").
  - Scope: access is checked by project membership: project-specific keys tried first, then team keys (dual path).
  - Transport: presented as a static secret in X-API-Key; Inject API requires X-API-Key, MCP accepts X-API-Key.
- OAuth tokens
  - Token handling: tokens use a snipara_at_ prefix and are validated via DB lookup + expiry check ("Token format | `snipara_at_` prefix"; "Validation | DB lookup + expiry check").
  - Scopes: read/write scope enforcement is applied ("Scopes | Read/write scope enforcement").
  - Device flow: "OAuth Device Flow (RFC 8628) enables secure MCP authentication without copying API keys" — i.e., device flow avoids distributing raw API keys.
  - Refresh/revoke: supports refresh tokens and token refresh endpoint; tokens can be listed and revoked via GET /api/settings/tokens and DELETE /api/settings/tokens/[tokenId].
  - Local storage/priority: the MCP client will check for a stored OAuth token in ~/.snipara/tokens.json first and fall back to API key from env if not present (so stored tokens are used preferentially).

Other relevant security notes from docs
- Invalid/expired OAuth tokens require re-authentication ("Invalid or expired OAuth token. Re-authenticate ...").
- You can remove ~/.snipara/tokens.json to force using an API key instead (example commands shown).

If you want a direct pointer to where each behavior is documented, see the auth, MCP, and OAuth Device Flow sections referenced above.

Available options (from the docs)
- Project API Key (X-API-Key: rlm_...)
- Team API Key (X-API-Key: rlm_...)
- OAuth access token via Device Flow (Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...)

Where each is used
- MCP endpoints accept either API Key or OAuth bearer token.
- Inject API endpoints accept only X-API-Key.

How they differ in security (documented facts)
- API keys
  - Format and storage: keys use an rlm_ prefix; the server hashes the key with SHA‑256 and looks up the hash in the database (auth.py:29–95).  
  - Scope: there are project-scoped keys (project-only) and team keys (access all projects in a team). The server tries project keys first, then falls back to team keys (validate_api_key()).  
  - Exposure controls: only the first 8 characters are shown in UI/logs and only the prefix is logged (so full key is not printed).  
  - Transport: sent in the X-API-Key header.
- OAuth tokens (Device Flow)
  - Format and validation: tokens have a snipara_at_ prefix; validation is a DB lookup plus expiry check and scope enforcement (auth.py:359–400).  
  - Rotation / refresh: standard refresh flow supported (POST /api/oauth/token with grant_type=refresh_token) returning a new access_token and refresh_token.  
  - Revocation/management: tokens can be listed (GET /api/settings/tokens) and revoked (DELETE /api/settings/tokens/[tokenId]).  
  - Usability/security benefit: device flow (RFC 8628) enables authenticating without copying API keys (users authenticate in the browser), reducing the need to distribute long‑lived secrets.  
  - Transport: sent as Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
- Implementation/priority behavior
  - MCP client behavior: the client checks for a stored OAuth token at ~/.snipara/tokens.json first, and falls back to an API key from environment variables if no token is present. (Removing tokens.json forces use of API key.)  
  - Invalid/expired OAuth tokens produce a 401 with a re‑auth message.

Summary (security tradeoffs from the docs)
- OAuth tokens provide scoped, expirable credentials with refresh and explicit revocation endpoints—better for short‑lived, revocable access and avoiding copying API keys (device flow).  
- API keys are protected server‑side by hashing and have UI/logging exposure minimization and project vs team scoping; they are used where OAuth is not supported (Inject API).  

If you need an aspect not described above (for example explicit key expiry policy for API keys), Not found in documentation.

Authentication options (from the docs)

1. Project API Key / Team API Key
- Header: X-API-Key: rlm_...
- Key format: rlm_ prefix; server hashes the key with SHA-256 and looks up the hash in the ApiKey table (project keys tried first, then team keys).
- Display/logging: only first 8 chars shown in UI/logs.
- Usage: recommended for project or team access; Inject API endpoint requires X-API-Key.
- Security notes from docs: keys are stored/compared as SHA-256 hashes in the DB.

2. OAuth Access Token (Device Flow / MCP)
- Header: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
- Token format: snipara_at_ prefix.
- Validation: DB lookup + expiry check; scopes enforced (read/write).
- Device flow: standard OAuth 2.0 device flow (RFC 8628) — enables MCP authentication without copying API keys (CLI -> browser code entry).
- Refresh: refresh token flow supported (POST /api/oauth/token with grant_type=refresh_token).
- Tokens may be stored locally in ~/.snipara/tokens.json (client checks this first); tokens can be listed (/api/settings/tokens) and revoked (DELETE /api/settings/tokens/[tokenId]).

How they differ in security (as stated in docs)
- Storage: API keys are hashed (SHA-256) in the DB; OAuth tokens are validated via DB lookup and expiry checks.
- Granularity: OAuth tokens have scopes (read/write) enforced; API key access is resolved by project/team keys.
- Usability/security tradeoff called out: the OAuth device flow is described as “secure” because it avoids copying API keys (CLI + browser flow).
- Priority/usage: MCP server supports both methods (checks for stored OAuth token first, then falls back to API key). Inject API only uses X-API-Key.

If you need exact endpoints, headers, or examples for each flow, they are listed in the documentation.

- Shared context is merged with project docs and the token budget is split between shared vs. project scope before returning results. (MCP Query Order: shared context is loaded first, then local project docs use the remaining budget.)

- Category-priority determines how shared tokens are distributed. Document categories (priority order) and example percentage splits shown in the docs:
  - MANDATORY — highest priority (example: 40%)
  - BEST_PRACTICES — high (example: 30%)
  - GUIDELINES — medium (example: 20%)
  - REFERENCE — low (example: 10%)

- Query-level parameter: rlm_context_query supports include_shared_context and shared_context_budget_percent (example default shown: shared = 30% of budget, project = 70%).

- Backend allocation logic (allocateTokenBudget):
  - Uses a DEFAULT_ALLOCATION mapping (in code): project: 0.40, MANDATORY: 0.25, BEST_PRACTICES: 0.20, GUIDELINES: 0.10, REFERENCE: 0.05 (can be overridden via customAllocation).
  - Project docs are allocated first with floor(totalBudget * allocation.project).
  - Each shared category is allocated floor(totalBudget * allocation[category]) and docs are added until the category budget is exhausted.
  - Within a category, docs are sorted by relevanceScore then included until the budget is used; docs that don't fit are marked allocated: false with reason "exceeded budget".

- Hard limits and enforcement:
  - LIMITS include MAX_COLLECTIONS_PER_PROJECT, MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION, MAX_DOCS_PER_COLLECTION, MIN_RELEVANCE_THRESHOLD.
  - enforceCollectionLimits will truncate a collection if its total tokens exceed MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION.

(See rlm_shared_context examples, MCP Query Order, and apps/web/lib/services/token-budget.ts in the documentation for the exact examples and code shown above.)

- Query flow: rlm_context_query loads shared context first, then queries local project docs with the remaining token budget (MCP Query Order). You can control the shared/project split with parameters like shared_context_budget_percent.

- Shared context merging: linked collections are merged and their documents are grouped by category (MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE). Categories determine priority.

- Budget splits (two places in the docs):
  - High-level/shared-context examples and the rlm_shared_context return example use: MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10%.
  - The token-budget implementation (apps/web/lib/services/token-budget.ts DEFAULT_ALLOCATION) treats project docs as a first-class allocation (project: 40%) and allocates the rest to shared categories as: MANDATORY 25%, BEST_PRACTICES 20%, GUIDELINES 10%, REFERENCE 5%.

- How allocation is applied (implementation details in token-budget.ts):
  - Project docs are allocated first using the configured project budget.
  - Shared docs are allocated per-category using their category budget.
  - Within each bucket, documents are sorted by relevanceScore (if present) and included until the category budget is exhausted; documents that don’t fit are marked “exceeded budget.”
  - Collection hard limits are enforced (e.g., MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION = 100,000); collections exceeding limits are truncated to fit.

(Quoted percentages and behavior are taken from the provided documentation and the token-budget implementation.)

- Shared context is merged with project docs and the token budget is split between project-specific docs and shared collection categories (MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE).

- Document/category-level percentages shown in the docs:
  - Document Categories table / shared-context examples: MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10%.
  - Token-budget implementation (apps/web/lib/services/token-budget.ts) DEFAULT_ALLOCATION: project 40%, MANDATORY 25%, BEST_PRACTICES 20%, GUIDELINES 10%, REFERENCE 5% (can be overridden via customAllocation).

- Allocation algorithm (allocateTokenBudget):
  - Groups docs into project docs and shared docs by category.
  - Computes projectBudget = floor(totalBudget * allocation.project) and allocates project docs first.
  - For each shared category, computes categoryBudget = floor(totalBudget * allocation[category]) and allocates within that budget.
  - allocateWithinBudget sorts docs by relevanceScore (if present) and includes documents until the category/project budget is exhausted; remaining docs are marked allocated: false with allocationReason: "exceeded budget".

- rlm_context_query behavior (query order):
  - Shared best-practices context is queried first, then local project scope is queried with the remaining token budget. (Example parameter: shared_context_budget_percent, default shown as 30 → shared first, project gets remaining 70.)

- Hard limits that affect allocation:
  - MAX_COLLECTIONS_PER_PROJECT: 10
  - MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION: 100_000 (enforced by enforceCollectionLimits which truncates a collection to fit)
  - MAX_DOCS_PER_COLLECTION: 50
  - MIN_RELEVANCE_THRESHOLD: 0.2

(These points are taken from the Shared Context, MCP Query Order, and token-budget.ts documentation.)

When the monthly query limit is exceeded the API returns a 429 Too Many Requests response with rate-limit details. Example from the docs:

{
  "success": false,
  "error": "Monthly query limit exceeded",
  "usage": {
    "current": 101,
    "max": 100,
    "exceeded": true,
    "reset_at": "2026-02-01T00:00:00Z"
  }
}

The `usage` fields are:
- `current`: current usage count
- `max`: maximum allowed queries
- `exceeded`: whether the limit has been reached
- `reset_at`: ISO 8601 timestamp when the limit resets

When a user exceeds their monthly query limit the API returns an HTTP 429 Too Many Requests response with rate‑limit details. Example from the docs:

{
  "success": false,
  "error": "Monthly query limit exceeded",
  "usage": {
    "current": 101,
    "max": 100,
    "exceeded": true,
    "reset_at": "2026-02-01T00:00:00Z"
  }
}

The `usage` fields are:
- `current`: current usage this billing period  
- `max`: maximum allowed queries  
- `exceeded`: whether the limit has been reached  
- `reset_at`: ISO 8601 timestamp when the limit resets

(Other related error responses shown in the docs include a `RATE_LIMIT_EXCEEDED` error message and an `INSUFFICIENT_CREDITS` error for query/credit limits.)

When a user exceeds their monthly query limit the API returns a 429 Too Many Requests response with rate-limit details. Example from the docs:

{
  "success": false,
  "error": "Monthly query limit exceeded",
  "usage": {
    "current": 101,
    "max": 100,
    "exceeded": true,
    "reset_at": "2026-02-01T00:00:00Z"
  }
}

Field meanings:
- current: Current usage count this billing period
- max: Maximum allowed queries (-1 for unlimited)
- exceeded: Whether the limit has been reached
- reset_at: ISO 8601 timestamp when the monthly limit resets
"Provide helpful messages when no data" — show an empty state. Use padding (`py-8`) rather than fixed heights, and include action buttons when appropriate.

The docs show two relevant points:

- The Context Query implementation will simply return no content: the response contains an empty "sections" list, total_tokens = 0, truncated = false, session_context may be None, and the handler still returns any suggestions produced by _generate_suggestions(query, selected_sections). (See the _handle_context_query response construction.)

- The UI guidance for empty states: show a helpful message when there is no data, use padding (py-8) rather than fixed heights, and include action buttons where appropriate. (See "4. Empty States".)

The docs show the context-query handler returns an empty result rather than an error. Concretely, if no sections are selected the response will contain:
- "sections": [] (no selected_sections)
- "total_tokens": 0
- "truncated": false (0 >= max_tokens is false)
- "session_context": (existing session context or null)
- "suggestions": generated by _generate_suggestions(query, selected_sections)

UI guidance (Empty States) additionally recommends showing a helpful message, using padding (py-8), and including action buttons when no data is available.
